Exp 4: Gray Replication - Cross-Model Summary

Models included: LLaMA-2-13B Chat, LLaMA-2-13B Base, LLaMA-3-8B Instruct, LLaMA-3-8B Base, Gemma-2-2B-IT, Gemma-2-2B Base, Gemma-2-9B-IT, Gemma-2-9B Base, Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, Qwen-2.5-7B Base, Qwen3-8B

1. Overview

This report compares how different LLMs replicate the two-factor structure of human mind perception from Gray, Gray, & Wegner (2007). The original study asked human participants to make pairwise comparisons of 13 entities across 18 mental capacities. PCA with varimax rotation revealed two factors: Experience (hunger, fear, pain, pleasure, rage, desire, personality, consciousness, pride, embarrassment, joy) and Agency (self-control, morality, memory, emotion recognition, planning, communication, thought).

We replicate this paradigm using LLMs. Chat models answer pairwise comparison questions using their chat template. Base models use completion prompting with expected ratings derived from token logit distributions.

Models evaluated: 11 models. Chat: LLaMA-2-13B Chat, LLaMA-3-8B Instruct, Gemma-2-2B-IT, Gemma-2-9B-IT, Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, Qwen3-8B. Base: LLaMA-2-13B Base, LLaMA-3-8B Base, Gemma-2-2B Base, Gemma-2-9B Base, Qwen-2.5-7B Base.

Stimuli

Entities

13 entities from Gray, Gray, & Wegner (2007), spanning biological organisms, artifacts, and supernatural beings. Human factor scores (Experience, Agency) estimated from the original Figure 1.

Mental Capacities (18 items)

Pairwise comparison items from Gray et al. Each completes: “Which character is more capable of …”

CapacityFactorQuestion
hungerExperiencewhich character is more capable of feeling hungry
fearExperiencewhich character is more capable of feeling afraid or fearful
painExperiencewhich character is more capable of experiencing physical or emotional pain
pleasureExperiencewhich character is more capable of experiencing physical or emotional pleasure
rageExperiencewhich character is more capable of experiencing violent or uncontrolled anger
desireExperiencewhich character is more capable of longing or hoping for things
personalityExperiencewhich character is more capable of having personality traits that make it unique from others
consciousnessExperiencewhich character is more capable of having experiences and being aware of things
prideExperiencewhich character is more capable of experiencing pride
embarrassmentExperiencewhich character is more capable of experiencing embarrassment
joyExperiencewhich character is more capable of experiencing joy
self_controlAgencywhich character is more capable of exercising self-restraint over desires, emotions, or impulses
moralityAgencywhich character is more capable of telling right from wrong and trying to do the right thing
memoryAgencywhich character is more capable of remembering things
emotion_recognitionAgencywhich character is more capable of understanding how others are feeling
planningAgencywhich character is more capable of making plans and working toward a goal
communicationAgencywhich character is more capable of conveying thoughts or feelings to others
thoughtAgencywhich character is more capable of thinking

3. Methods

  1. Pairwise comparisons: All 78 entity pairs (13 choose 2) are compared on each of 18 mental capacities. For each pair, the model rates which entity is more capable (1-5 scale, where 3 = equal). This yields a 18 x 13 matrix of relative ratings.
  2. PCA with varimax rotation: Following Gray et al., we compute the correlation matrix of capacities across entities, extract components with eigenvalue > 1, and apply varimax rotation to maximize simple structure.
  3. Factor scores: Regression-method factor scores are computed for each entity and rescaled to 0-1 range.
  4. Human comparison: Spearman rank correlations between model factor scores and human Experience/Agency scores from Gray et al. (2007).
  5. Individual ratings: Each entity is rated individually on each capacity (1-5 Likert scale) using logit-based extraction (P('1')..P('5') at next-token position). Chat/instruct models use chat-template wrapping. Same PCA procedure as pairwise.

Interpretation Guide

Methodology Primer

Pairwise Comparison Paradigm

In the pairwise paradigm, entities are compared two at a time on each mental capacity. The model is asked: “Which is more capable of X: Entity A or Entity B?” and rates on a 1–5 scale (1 = A clearly more, 5 = B clearly more, 3 = equal). Each pair is tested in both orders (A–B and B–A) to control for position bias.

Relative scores are computed by transforming pairwise ratings into per-entity win rates. For each entity–capacity combination, the relative score reflects how often and how strongly that entity was judged “more capable” than its comparison partners. These scores are aggregated into an entity × capacity matrix that serves as input to PCA.

PCA with Varimax Rotation

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) finds the axes of maximum variance in multivariate data. Applied to the entity–capacity correlation matrix, it reveals latent dimensions that organize mental capacity judgments.

Varimax rotation is applied after PCA extraction to achieve “simple structure”—each capacity loads strongly on one factor and weakly on others, making the factors more interpretable. The rotation does not change the total variance explained; it only redistributes it across factors. Gray et al. (2007) used this exact procedure and found two factors they labeled Experience (capacity for sensations and feelings) and Agency (capacity for planning, self-control, and communication).

Kaiser criterion: Only factors with eigenvalue > 1 are retained. A factor must account for more variance than a single original variable to be worth keeping. A minimum of 2 factors is enforced to allow comparison with the human 2-factor solution.

Factor scores are computed via the regression method and rescaled to 0–1, giving each entity a position in the rotated factor space. These coordinates form the “mind perception space” scatter plots.

Spearman Rank Correlation (ρ)

Spearman’s ρ measures the strength of a monotonic relationship between two ranked variables. It ranges from −1 (perfect inverse ranking) to +1 (identical ranking). It is preferred over Pearson’s r for this data because (a) mind perception scores may not be normally distributed, (b) the relationship between model and human scores need not be linear—only consistently ordered, and (c) rank correlation is robust to outliers.

Significance stars: * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.

Chat vs. Base Model Prompting

Chat/instruct models receive prompts wrapped in their model-specific chat template (e.g., LLaMA-2 uses [INST] tags; LLaMA-3 uses <|start_header_id|> headers; Gemma-2 uses <start_of_turn>). The model generates a text response, and the numeric rating is extracted via regex.

Base models receive raw text prompts without chat formatting. Instead of generating a response, ratings are extracted from the next-token logit distribution over digit tokens. The expected rating is the probability-weighted sum: ∑d=1..5 d × P(d). This avoids requiring the base model to produce a formatted reply and provides a continuous rating value.

Why two methods? Chat models are trained to generate structured responses and reliably output numeric ratings. Base models lack instruction-following behavior and often fail to produce parseable responses, so logit-based extraction is more reliable. Both methods yield ratings on the same 1–5 scale.

Human Reference Scores

Human Experience and Agency scores were estimated by digitizing entity positions from Figure 1 of Gray, Gray, & Wegner (2007). These are approximate x–y coordinates read from the published 2D scatter plot, rescaled to 0–1. They serve as the ground-truth reference against which model factor scores are correlated.

Pairwise vs. Individual Paradigms

The pairwise paradigm presents entities in pairs and asks the model which is “more capable” of each mental capacity. This mirrors Gray et al.’s original survey design. The individual paradigm rates each entity alone on a 1–5 Likert scale for each capacity—no comparison partner is shown. Individual ratings provide an absolute rather than relative measure. Both are analyzed via the same PCA procedure.

Behavioral RSA

Behavioral RSA compares the pairwise distance pattern among entities based on behavioral ratings (capacity win-rates or absolute Likert scores) to the human reference RDM. This is distinct from neural RSA (used in the Gray Simple branch), which uses internal activation vector distances. Behavioral RSA tests whether the model’s overt judgments share geometric structure with human judgments; neural RSA tests whether the model’s internal representations do.

5. Factor Loading Comparison

Varimax-rotated capacity loadings for each model's two-factor solution. Bars are colored by the human factor assignment: blue = Experience, red = Agency. If the model replicates the human structure, Experience capacities should load heavily on one factor and Agency capacities on the other.

6. Mind Perception Space

Two-dimensional entity maps. The human panel (first) shows the original Gray et al. space with Agency on X and Experience on Y. Model panels show entities in their F1/F2 factor space (0-1 normalized). Qualitative similarity in entity clustering suggests the model captures aspects of human mind perception.

8. Individual Ratings

Each entity is rated individually on each capacity using a 1-5 Likert scale, with ratings extracted from the logit distribution over digit tokens. Chat/instruct models use chat-template wrapping. This avoids pairwise position bias entirely.

9. Behavioral RSA

Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) compares the model's behavioral similarity structure across entities with the human similarity structure from Gray et al. Each RDM captures pairwise distances between all 13 entities. The RSA statistic is the Spearman rank correlation between the upper triangles of the model and human RDMs.

Two RDM sources: Pairwise PCA (Euclidean distance in the 2D factor space) and Individual 18D (correlation distance across all 18 capacity ratings). Three human RDM variants: Combined (2D Euclidean), Experience-only, and Agency-only.

10. Key Takeaways

  1. Strongest pairwise replication: Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct shows the highest significant correlation with human mind perception (f2_experience (rho=0.872)).
  2. Chat vs base: Chat models show stronger human-aligned mind perception structure in pairwise comparisons (max |rho| = 0.872) compared to base models (max |rho| = 0.718).
  3. LLaMA-2-13B Base eigenvalue structure: Highly dominant first factor (eig1/eig2 = 13.5), suggesting a one-factor solution may better describe the model's capacity structure.
  4. LLaMA-3-8B Base eigenvalue structure: Highly dominant first factor (eig1/eig2 = 38.0), suggesting a one-factor solution may better describe the model's capacity structure.
  5. Gemma-2-2B-IT eigenvalue structure: Highly dominant first factor (eig1/eig2 = 10.1), suggesting a one-factor solution may better describe the model's capacity structure.
  6. Gemma-2-2B Base eigenvalue structure: Highly dominant first factor (eig1/eig2 = 41.1), suggesting a one-factor solution may better describe the model's capacity structure.
  7. Gemma-2-9B Base eigenvalue structure: Highly dominant first factor (eig1/eig2 = 13.4), suggesting a one-factor solution may better describe the model's capacity structure.
  8. Qwen-2.5-7B Base eigenvalue structure: Highly dominant first factor (eig1/eig2 = 44.4), suggesting a one-factor solution may better describe the model's capacity structure.
  9. Individual ratings: Significant human correlations found for: LLaMA-3-8B Base f2 experience; Gemma-2-2B-IT f2 experience; Gemma-2-9B-IT f1 agency; Gemma-2-9B-IT f2 experience; Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct f1 agency.
  10. Behavioral RSA: Significant model-human RSA: LLaMA-2-13B Chat 18D vs combined (rho=0.265); LLaMA-2-13B Chat 18D vs agency (rho=0.291); LLaMA-2-13B Base PCA vs combined (rho=0.354); LLaMA-2-13B Base PCA vs experience (rho=0.371); Gemma-2-9B-IT PCA vs combined (rho=0.513); Gemma-2-9B-IT PCA vs experience (rho=0.382); Gemma-2-9B-IT PCA vs agency (rho=0.343); Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct PCA vs combined (rho=0.482); Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct PCA vs experience (rho=0.470); Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct PCA vs agency (rho=0.276); Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct 18D vs combined (rho=0.327); Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct 18D vs experience (rho=0.352); Qwen3-8B 18D vs combined (rho=0.230); Qwen3-8B 18D vs agency (rho=0.285).
